1: A theme: Psalm 59 is a Michtam of David when Saul sent men to watch the house and kill him (1 Samuel 19:11-17). The historical specificity of the superscription makes this a "David as fugitive" psalm. "Rescue me from my enemies, O my God; set me beyond the reach of those who rise up against me." The prayer for rescue from organized, state-sponsored assassination is the prayer that Christ's community prays under persecution. Hebrews 13:6: "So we say with confidence, 'The Lord is my helper; I will not be afraid. What can mere mortals do to me?'" Christ himself was the object of organized state violence (Pilate + Herod + Sanhedrin) — and was rescued through the resurrection.
2: A theme: "Rescue me from those who do evil, and save me from men who want my blood." The bloodthirst of the evildoers — men who specifically "want my blood (ʾanšê dāmîm)" — is the Passion's language. Matthew 27:25: "His blood is on us and on our children!" — the very blood that the crowd invokes is the blood of the new covenant (Matthew 26:28: "This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins"). The men who wanted Christ's blood, in demanding it, unwittingly invoked its redemptive power upon themselves.
3: A type: "For see — they lie in ambush for my life; fierce men are gathering against me — not for any offense or sin of mine, LORD." The declaration of innocence — no offense or sin on the Psalmist's part justifying the attack — is fulfilled literally in Christ. John 19:4: "Pilate announced again, 'Look, I am bringing him out to you to let you know that I find no basis for a charge against him.'" Luke 23:47: "the centurion... said, 'Surely this was a righteous man.'" The innocence-before-attackers of Psalms 59:3 is not merely David's aspirational claim but Christ's literal truth: no offense, no sin, yet surrounded.
4: A type: "For no fault of mine they have mobilized against me. Arouse yourself, come to meet me, and look!" The prayer "arouse yourself, come to meet me" is the prayer for divine waking — YHWH rousing himself from apparent inactivity in the face of innocent suffering. This prayer appears to be unanswered at the cross — God seems asleep. But Acts 2:24: "God raised him from the dead, freeing him from the agony of death, because it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him." The divine self-rousing that the Psalm prays for is the resurrection — YHWH "coming to meet" the innocent one entombed by his enemies.
5: A revelation of God: "You are the LORD God of hosts, the God of Israel — rouse yourself to punish all the nations; show no mercy to those who commit treason with evil intent." The invocation of YHWH Ṣĕbāʾôt ("LORD of hosts/armies") is the divine warrior title that grounds the prayer for just punishment of treacherous nations. The NT situates this judgment in Christ: Revelation 19:11-16 presents Christ as the divine warrior on the white horse, "KING OF KINGS AND LORD OF LORDS" — the YHWH of hosts who now rides in final judgment through his anointed Son. The nations who committed treason against the Messiah face the LORD of hosts through Christ.
6: A shadow: "They return each evening, howling like dogs and circling around the city." The nocturnal surveillance — prowling, howling, circling — is the imagery of predatory persistent threat. John 18:3: "So Judas came to the garden, guiding a detachment of soldiers and some officials from the chief priests and the Pharisees. They were carrying torches, lanterns and weapons." The evening arrival at Gethsemane with torches and weapons is the fulfillment of the nocturnal predators of Psalms 59:6 — they come at night, circling the place of prayer.
7: A shadow: "Listen — they pour out venom with their mouths; their lips are full of swords, for they think, 'Who is going to hear?'" The self-confident secrecy of those who conspire with weapon-words — "who is going to hear?" — is the arrogance of those who believe their plans are hidden from YHWH. Matthew 26:3-4 records the Sanhedrin plotting in the high priest's palace — in secret, they believe. But Acts 2:23 reveals the divine surveillance: God's "deliberate plan and foreknowledge" covered even this secret conspiracy. The "who is going to hear?" of Psalms 59:7 is answered: YHWH heard and incorporated it into the redemptive plan.
8: A revelation of God: "But you, LORD, laugh at them; you scoff at all the nations." The divine laughter at those who conspire against the anointed is the exact phrase of Psalms 2:4 ("He who sits in the heavens laughs; the Lord scoffs at them"). Psalm 2 is explicitly Messianic (Psalms 2:2 "against the LORD and against his Anointed"). The divine laugh in Psalms 59:8 at those who surrounded David with weapons is the same divine scoff at those who surrounded Christ with torches and arrested him — in the absolute confidence that YHWH's plan would not be derailed by human weapons and conspiracies.
9: A revelation of God: "O my Strength, I will wait on you; for God is my fortress." The divine title "my Strength" (ʿuzzî) and "my fortress" (miśgabbî) establishes YHWH as the source of both active power and defensive security. 2 Corinthians 12:9-10: "My power is made perfect in weakness... For when I am weak, then I am strong." The paradox that Paul articulates — weakness as the context for divine strength — is the shape of Christ's entire ministry: the vulnerable incarnate one waiting on the Father, whose power is made perfect through and despite weakness.
10: A revelation of God: "The God of my steadfast love will come ahead of me; God will let me look in triumph on my enemies." The divine precedence — YHWH going ahead of the Psalmist — and the promised triumph-view over enemies is the covenant military pattern. The resurrection is the divine going-ahead: Christ rises before the disciples see him; the angels precede the disciples to the tomb (Matthew 28:6: "He has risen, just as he said"). Romans 8:37: "in all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us." The God who "comes ahead" to grant triumph is the Father who raised Christ first, enabling the community's triumph through him.
11: A shadow: "Do not kill them, so my people will not forget; bring them down by your power and humble them, O Lord our shield!" The prayer to humiliate rather than annihilate — so the lesson is not lost — is the Psalm's pedagogical approach to justice. The NT's vision of eschatological judgment includes a memorial function: Revelation 20:12, "the dead were judged according to what they had done as recorded in the books." The divine remembering and recording that makes judgment pedagogically meaningful is the same divine attentiveness that ensures the enemies of the Messiah do not simply disappear — their accountability stands.
12: A shadow: "For the sin of their mouths and the words of their lips, let them be ensnared in their own pride. For the cursing and deception that they utter." The judgment corresponding to weapon-speech — "ensnared in their own pride" — is the justice principle of Proverbs 18:7: "The mouths of fools are their undoing." At the trial of Jesus, the false witnesses contradict each other (Mark 14:56-59), the high priest tears his robes in a forbidden act (Leviticus 21:10, making him ritually impure), and Pilate's verdict is written in three languages as a permanent testimony (John 19:20). The conspirators are repeatedly ensnared by their own words and actions.
13: A revelation of God: "Consume them in your wrath; consume them until they are no more, so that people may know that God rules over Jacob to the ends of the earth." The eschatological consumption of the enemies is not vindictiveness but revelation: the goal is that all the earth know that God rules. Acts 17:31 frames the judgment through Christ as the demonstration of divine governance: "He has given proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead." The resurrection is the "so that people may know that God rules" of Psalms 59:13 — the proof of YHWH's sovereignty enacted through the Messiah.
14: A shadow: "They return each evening, howling like dogs and circling the city." The refrain of v6 repeated is the Psalm's structural reminder that the threat is persistent — it returns each evening, not a one-time crisis. The sustained, returning opposition to Christ across his ministry (John 7:1, Psalms 7:32, Psalms 8:59, Psalms 10:31, Psalms 11:53) is this same pattern: each new encounter brings renewed attempts to arrest or kill him. The howling-dogs-circling-city is the accumulated hostility that the Gospels chronicle over three years of ministry.
15: A theme: "They roam around in search of food; they growl when they are not satisfied." The predatory unsatisfied hunger of the enemies — prowling, growling without satiation — is the portrait of those whose hunger for power and control cannot be fulfilled. Luke 23:23: "But with loud shouts they insistently demanded that he be crucified, and their shouts prevailed." The crowd's insatiable demand — demanding more (crucifixion after flogging, thorns, and mockery were not enough) — is the unsatisfied hunger of Psalms 59:15, whose appetite grows with feeding.
16: A revelation of God: "But I will sing of your strength; I will sing aloud of your steadfast love in the morning; for you have been my fortress and my place of refuge in my day of distress." The morning singing of divine strength and steadfast love — the dawn of praise after the night of howling enemies — is the resurrection-morning image. Mark 16:2: "Very early on the first day of the week, just after sunrise, they were on their way to the tomb." The morning of the resurrection is the "morning" of Psalms 59:16 — the dawn after the dark night of the Passion in which the community sings of strength and steadfast love.
17: A revelation of God: "O my Strength, I will sing praise to you; for God is my fortress, the God who shows me steadfast love." The closing declaration — God as Strength, Fortress, and Steadfast Love — completes the Psalm with a triple assertion of divine character against which no enemy prevails. These three divine attributes are embodied in Christ: 1 Corinthians 1:24 ("Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God"), Ephesians 2:14 ("he himself is our peace"), and Romans 8:39 ("nothing will be able to separate us from the love of God that is in Christ Jesus our Lord"). The Psalm's closing divine portrait is the character of the Father as expressed in the Son.
